title: Multi-Agent Systems
description: Supervisor patterns, specialist agents, planner-executor designs, communication, debugging, and anti-patterns.

questions.title: When should you use a multi-agent system?
questions.scenario: A team proposes five agents for a simple FAQ assistant.
questions.expectedAnswer: Use multi-agent systems when tasks benefit from specialization, parallel work, independent critique, or separate responsibilities. Avoid them when a single workflow or model call is enough. Multi-agent designs add coordination overhead, latency, cost, debugging difficulty, and emergent failure modes. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A team proposes five agents for a simple FAQ assistant. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by mapping the concept to a concrete workflow, choosing the simplest suitable technique, validating it with data or user feedback, and explaining how it changes the product or platform behavior.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "When should you use a multi-agent system?", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real multi-agent systems setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Explain the supervisor agent pattern.
questions.scenario: A research assistant needs search, summarization, fact-checking, and report-writing specialists.
questions.expectedAnswer: A supervisor agent routes tasks to specialist agents, monitors progress, aggregates results, and decides when work is complete. It centralizes coordination but can become a bottleneck or single point of failure. Strong answers include routing criteria, state management, guardrails, and tracing. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A research assistant needs search, summarization, fact-checking, and report-writing specialists. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for multi-agent systems.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Explain the supervisor agent pattern.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real multi-agent systems setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: What is the planner-executor pattern?
questions.scenario: An agent must gather information, compare options, and produce a recommendation.
questions.expectedAnswer: The planner decomposes a task into steps while executor agents or tools perform each step. This separates strategy from action and improves controllability. Risks include bad plans, stale plans, excessive steps, and poor feedback between execution and replanning. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: How should agents communicate?
questions.scenario: Two specialist agents keep debating and never produce a final answer.
questions.expectedAnswer: Agents should communicate through structured messages with task state, assumptions, outputs, confidence, and required next actions. Avoid unbounded chat loops. Use schemas, shared memory, event logs, and explicit termination criteria to make communication testable. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: How do you debug multi-agent systems?
questions.scenario: A multi-agent workflow is expensive and occasionally returns contradictory conclusions.
questions.expectedAnswer: Debugging requires traces of messages, tool calls, state changes, decisions, costs, and outputs. Replayable runs, deterministic seeds where possible, scenario tests, per-agent metrics, and failure taxonomies help identify routing errors, loops, tool misuse, or context loss. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: How would you explain specialist agent design in a Multi-Agent Systems interview?

questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define specialist agent design, explain why it matters for multi-agent systems, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: How would you explain agent federation in a Multi-Agent Systems interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through agent federation for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define agent federation, explain why it matters for multi-agent systems, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through agent federation for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for multi-agent systems.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "How would you explain agent federation in a Multi-Agent Systems interview?", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real multi-agent systems setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

Skipping the scenario and giving a generic textbook explanation.

Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
